contest.title: 2024 ICPC Asia Taichung Regional Contest (Unrated, Online Mirror, ICPC Rules, Preferably Teams)

sources.notes: Read the complete statement, operation-order rule, examples, and constraints: n <= 10^6, 31-bit nonnegative values, and each prefix/suffix sort may be used at most once.

Read the official contest analysis linked from Codeforces. It splits disjoint and intersecting prefix/suffix ranges, suggests enumerating one boundary, and states that O(n) and O(n log n) implementations are possible.

Read the complete Luogu write-up. It stable-ranks duplicates into a permutation, scans both operation orders, and maintains the consecutive rank frontier with a heap and the last necessary operation endpoint.
Opened and read the linked submission, confirmed its Accepted verdict, and checked its stable-rank conversion, min/max-heap sweeps, mismatch boundaries, and 64-bit cost handling.

Derived the prefix-position formula and exhaustively matched it against direct simulation for every permutation through n=8 and every ternary array through n=7, including both operation orders and omitted operations.

annotation.summary: Sort an array using at most one prefix sort and at most one suffix sort, in either order, minimizing the sum of the squared chosen lengths.
annotation.constraints: 1 <= n <= 10^6; 0 <= s_i < 2^31-1. Costs reach 2*n^2, so 64-bit arithmetic is required. O(n log n) time and O(n) memory are acceptable.
annotation.core_idea: Replace equal values by stable ranks: sort pairs (value,original_index) and obtain a permutation p of 1..n; this preserves every prefix/suffix sorting feasibility because equal copies are interchangeable. For prefix-then-suffix, let k be the number of final smallest ranks left before the suffix [k+1,n]. Let mx[k] be the maximum original position of ranks 1..k, and bad[k] the last i<=k with p[i]!=i. If mx[k]>k, the operations overlap and the shortest feasible prefix has length x=mx[k], because all ranks 1..k must be brought into it. Otherwise the first k positions already contain exactly ranks 1..k, positions bad[k]+1..k are already fixed, and x=bad[k] suffices. Minimize x^2+(n-k)^2 over k=0..n. Apply the same sweep to q[i]=n+1-p[n+1-i] to cover suffix-then-prefix.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) time for comparison-based stable ranking plus O(n) for the two boundary sweeps, and O(n) memory. With linear-time radix sorting of the 31-bit keys, the whole method is O(n).
tricks: Stable occurrence ranks remove duplicate-value ambiguity without changing sortedness feasibility.
The intersecting case is summarized by the furthest position of a required small rank; the disjoint case is summarized by the last mismatch inside a closed prefix.
Reverse and complement the rank permutation to reuse the same scan for the opposite operation order.
k=0 or k=n naturally represents omitting one operation.

solution_variants.name: stable-rank-boundary-sweeps
solution_variants.summary: After stable ranking, precompute inverse positions, prefix maxima of those positions, and last mismatches. Enumerate every split k with the exact minimal prefix endpoint, then repeat on the reverse-complement permutation for the opposite order.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n) after rank normalization and O(n) memory; O(n log n) total with comparison sorting, or O(n) total with radix sorting.

solution_variants.name: stable-rank-heap-frontier-sweeps
solution_variants.summary: Scan left to right while a min-heap stores processed ranks above the largest completed consecutive prefix 1..lst; track the last position that must belong to the prefix operation and test the suffix length n-lst. Mirror right to left with a max-heap for the other operation order.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log n) time and O(n) memory, including stable rank normalization.

tags.evidence: For every split k, the exact minimum first-operation endpoint is determined by a prefix maximum of inverse rank positions and the last prefix mismatch; the mirrored sweep handles the suffix extrema.
The split k and the shortest feasible opposite endpoint advance as monotone boundaries, allowing every prefix/suffix pair to be represented and evaluated in one scan per operation order.
Sorting (value,index) pairs converts duplicates into a stable rank permutation whose identity order exactly represents nondecreasing value order.
For a fixed split, taking the shortest prefix or suffix that contains every required rank is always optimal because any longer operation only increases its squared cost.
The independent O(n log n) variant uses min- and max-heaps to consume newly available consecutive ranks and maintain the exact frontier under each directional sweep.
